coredata.dc:title: Rapid and facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots with high antioxidant activity
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xinjun
Ma, Xiao-Yan
Tian, Jianping
Huang, Zhixiong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of most promising antioxidants owing to their fascinating properties, including favorable biocompatibility, high antioxidant activity, and low toxicity. In this work, an efficient and rapid approach for preparing GQDs was developed. The graphene oxide (GO) sheet was thoroughly fragmented into the GQDs by the hydroxyl radicals (OH•) generated from a O3/H2O2/UV oxidation reaction. GQDs possessed a large content of sp2-hybridized carbon domains and abundant functional groups in edge. The 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH•) radical scavenging method and dye decoloration test were used to investigate the antioxidant activity of GQDs, which exhibited high antioxidant activity and great application prospects for corrosion protection, medicinal uses, and the antioxidation of organic dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Graphene quantum dots
Hydroxyl radical
Antioxidant activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of GQDs antioxidant activity and radical scavenging assays

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . TEM images of GO given different reaction times: (a) 0.5 h, and (b) 1 h; (c) TEM images of GQDs (Inset in panel c presents a high resolution TEM image of GQDs); (d)size distribution of GQDs.

. TEM images of GO given different reaction times: (a) 0.5 h, and (b) 1 h; (c,d) TEM images of GQDs (Inset in panel d presents a high resolution TEM image of GQDs); (e)size distribution of GQDs; (f)XRD patterns; (g) FT-IR spectra; (h) TGA curves.
. The deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of GO (a), and after the O3/H2O2/UV treatment for 1 h(b), 2 h(c), and 3 h(d).
. (a) UV–visible spectra of GQDs; (b) PL spectra of the aqueous suspension of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
. Raman spectra the GO and GQDs.
. The scavenging efficiencies of the DPPH radicals in H2O, GO, GQDs and Ascorbic acid (a), photocatalytic degradation of MB (b).

head.ce:title: Rapid and facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots with high antioxidant activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Provincial Key Lab of Process Equipment and Control
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of most promising antioxidants owing to their fascinating properties, including favorable biocompatibility, high antioxidant activity, and low toxicity. In this work, an efficient and rapid approach for preparing GQDs was developed. The graphene oxide (GO) sheet was thoroughly fragmented into the GQDs by the hydroxyl radicals (OH•) generated from a O3/H2O2/UV oxidation reaction. GQDs possessed a large content of sp2-hybridized carbon domains and abundant functional groups in edge. The 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH•) radical scavenging method and dye decoloration test were used to investigate the antioxidant activity of GQDs, which exhibited high antioxidant activity and great application prospects for corrosion protection, medicinal uses, and the antioxidation of organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), an important signaling molecule, play a crucial role in signal transduction and cell function regulation in a series of signaling pathways at normal physiological concentration. ROS accumulates excessively either because of excessive production or because it cannot be removed in time, which then causes oxidative damage to nucleic acids, proteins, unsaturated fatty acids, lipids in cell membranes, and soluble components outside of cells, resulting in oxidative stress injury [1]. Oxidative stress is closely related to aging and the onset of many diseases, including cancer, atherosclerosis, diabetes, and neurodegenerative diseases [2-4]. In the process of oxidation, antioxidants can either scavenge free radicals or prevent the production of free radicals, thereby protecting tissues from oxidative stress damage and improving the immuno-capacity of tissues. This is widely used in the fields of medicine, health care, cosmetics, and the food industry [5,6]. Carbon nanomaterials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon dots, have been explored as free-radical scavengers [7-9]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), small fragments of graphene sheets, are attracting much attention due to their outstanding hydrophilicity, unique optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. In particular, a GQDs consists of layers of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and some sp3-hybridized carbon atoms linked with oxygen-containing functional groups [10]. Surface functional groups’ strong hydrogen donation and large content of sp2-hybridized carbon domains endow them with excellent free radical scavenging properties. Consequently, developing effective strategies remains essential for the preparation of GQDs with antioxidant activity at the mass scale. In this work, a O3/H2O2/UV oxidation method was developed to prepare the mass scale production of GQDs. Likewise, GQDs’ surface morphology and structure were investigated. Prepared GQDs with highly graphitic structure and abundant functional groups exhibited extraordinarily high antioxidant activity. This work presents a novel, rapid, and scalable synthetic method for providing GQDs as efficient antioxidants.

Graphite powder (45 μm), potassium permanganate and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (shanghai, China). 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), L-ascorbic acid and ethanol were provided from Sigma Aldrich. Methylene blue(MB), hydrochloric acid and sulfuric acid were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from natural graphite powder in accordance with a modified Hummers’ method [11]. GQDs were prepared by a O3/H2O2/UV oxidation method. First, 0.10 g GO was added to 100 mL water in a 250 mL Quartz beaker, and dispersed through ultrasonication for 30 min. Afterward,10 mL 30% H2O2 was mixed with GO aqueous dispersion. A 120 W low-pressure Hg lamp was uesd as a source of ultraviolet (UV) light. The wavelength range of the UV lamp was 180–260 nm, and the main wavelengths were 185 nm and 254 nm. The UV light was positioned directly above the beaker at a the distance of 10 cm. Ozone was produced with corona discharge in an ozone generator (Beijing Tonglin Scitech Co., Ltd, China) from pure oxygen.The input rate of ozone was 3 g·h−1 and was delivered at a gas flow rate of 18L·h−1. The ozone was fed into the breakers through a bubble stone.

The atomic force microscope (AFM) was obtained by the Asylum Research MFP-3D™ in tapping-mode. The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded using a FEI Tecnai F30 microscope. The Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded using a Nicolet NEXUS 670 FT-IR spectrophotometert. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) spectra were carried out using a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD using a monochromatic Al Kα source at room temperature. Raman spectra were performed with a Horiba Jobin Yvon LABRAM-HR800 with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The PL spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600 instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of GQDs antioxidant activity and radical scavenging assays

ce:list-item.ce:para: DPPH• scavenging efficiency. DPPH• scavenging activity was obtained by the method described by wang et al. [10]. Typically, 2 mL DPPH (100 μM) absolute ethanol was mixed with 2 mL solution of GQDs (20 μg/mL). And after being incubated in dark conditions for 30 min, the absorption of solution at 517 nm was measured by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The DPPH• scavenging efficiency (%)= (Ac-AGQDs)/Ac × 100, AGQDs and Ac present the absorption intensity of DPPH• mixtures and the GQDs-free DPPH• solution, respectively. In addition, the control experiments, 2.0 mL H2O and 2.0 mL ascorbic acid solution (20 μg/mL) were conducted to study the DPPH• scavenging efficiency.

Preparation of GQDs-coated MB membranes. Nylon Membranes (Magna) with 0.22 μm pore size were first immersed in GQDs aqueous solutions (20 μg/mL) for 10 min. The membranes were thoroughly rinsed with DI water and dried at room temperature. Afterwards, GQDs-coated membranes were immersed in MB aqueous solutions (0.05 mg/mL) containing 0.01 M H2O2 for 90 min. After dried at room temperature, the GQDs-coated MB and GQDs-free MB membranes were irradiated by ultraviolet for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of the GO and GQDs were shown in Fig. 2f. The (0 0 1) diffraction peak in the XRD pattern of GO appeared at 10.4°, corresponding to the layer-to-layer distance of 0.84 nm. It is significantly larger than that of pristine graphite (0.34 nm) [11]. The XRD patterns of GQDs shows a broad peak at around 24° corresponding to the (002) peak which is matched with JCPDS Card No, 75–0444. The FT-IR spectra of GO and GQDs are shown in Fig. 2g. The peaks located at 3400, 1723, 1630, 1385, 1221 and 1055 cm−1 correspond to the –OH, –C=O, C=C, C–OH, O–H and C–O stretching, respectively [20]. The intensity of the peak (C–OH) of GQDs is decreased compared to that of GO, which indicates that –OH is removed partially in the reaction. It is was confirmed by XPS spectra. Fig. 3 h shows the thermogravimetric analysis of GQDs. 10% of initial weight of GQDs loss up to 120 °C that related to thermal desorption of physically adsorbed water molecules on GQDs surface. Main weight loss is occurred at 120–800 °C attributable to thermal desorption of oxygen-containing functional group such as –COOH and –OH groups [21].

XPS spectra was used to investigate the chemical composition variation of GO during the O3/H2O2/UV oxidation reaction. Fig. 3 presents the deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of the GO, and those after O3/H2O2/UV treatment for 1, 2, and 3 h. The peak at 288.2 eV is attributed to carboxylic group (COOH) and increased gradually with the increasing reaction time, implying that more COOH groups were generated during the oxidation reaction. The peak at 287.6 eV is attributed to carbonyl group (C=O) and the intensity decreased dramatically. While the intensity of the peak at 286.5 eV, corresponds to alkoxy group (C-O) in how it initially increased and then decreased, the peak at 284.4 eV is attributed to C=C/C-C in aromatic rings [17].The reason might be that, in the initial stage of the reaction, there were a large number of C=C bands in the base plane of GO, which provided abundant attack sites for •HO electrophilic addition and resulted in an increase of the relative amount of alkoxy group. Hydroxyl radicals are one of the most powerful oxidizing agents, capable of converting oxygen-containing groups to carboxyl. Ultimately, a fraction of carboxyl groups was eliminated via decarboxylation with the release of CO/CO2. This resulted in the relative amount of alkoxy group and carbonyl group were decreased [20]. Then, the oxidation process would lead to the breaking of carbon–carbon bonds and generation of small organic molecules and carbon dioxide. Hence, the large π-conjugated skeleton with micro-meter lateral size was cut into small oxidized graphene quantum dots [17].

To evaluate the optical properties of GQDs, the UV–visible absorption and PL spectral analysis of GQDs were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, GQDs has an obvious absorption peak at 240 nm, which corresponds to the π–π* transition of aromatic –C=C– [22]. Fig. 4b shows PL spectra of the aqueous suspension of GQDs, the emission peaks of GQDs shows red-shifts with a change in excitation from 300 to 460 nm. And the maximum excitation wavelengths of GQDs was 340 nm. The Raman spectroscopy is a versatile tool for studying the atomic structure of carbon materials, Fig. 5 show the Raman spectroscopy of GO and GQDs, the D peak around at 1350 cm−1 correspond to the disordered sp3 carbon atoms, where the G peak around at 1580 cm−1 are ascribed to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in the graphitic layers, the intensity ratio of D/G peak (ID/IG) is ofen considered as a measure of the integrity of the graphite structure, the ID/IG ratio of GO is 0.9792, and the ratio of GQDs decrease to 0.8998, which indicates the a increase in the fraction of aromatic sp2 domains and a few disordered sp3 carbon atoms were removed by the oxidation process.

1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH•) radical scavenging, an incredibly commonly method for evaluating antioxidant activity, was first proposed by Blois in 1958 [23]. DPPH• scavenging activity and efficiency were obtained via the method described by Wang et al. [10]. GQDs’ antioxidant activity was investigated via the DPPH• radical scavenging method. Fig. 6 a shows that the scavenging efficiency of H2O was 18.5%, GO was 42%, GQDs was 79%, and ascorbic acid was 82%. GQDs show high antioxidant activity and are comparable to ascorbic acid [7]. Dye coloration of the GQDs-MB membranes was used to further evaluate the antioxidant activity of GQDs [24]. A similar procedure to that reported by Ruiz et al was used [25]. Photocatalytic degradation of MB organic dyes on bare and GQDs-coated membranes under UV light irradiation was conducted. Fig. 6b shows the color change of the MB membranes both with and without GQDs coating after they were exposed to UV light for 90 min. The decoloration of MB on uncoated membranes occurred much faster than the GQDs-coated membranes after the same irradiation time, which also indicates the highly radical scavenging activity of the GQDs that could be used as a protective coating for organic dyes against oxidative degradation by reactive oxygen species. The main mechanism causing the radical inhibition of GQDs was a combination of the hydrogen donation from oxygen-containing functional groups and the adduct formation in the sp2 hybridized carbon domains [26-28]. XPS and TEM together reveal that GQDs prepared by the O3/H2O2/UV oxidation method have a large content of sp2-hybridized carbon domains and abundant functional groups in edge, therefore GQDs show high antioxidant activity.

In summary, the O3/H2O2/UV oxidation process, a novel and facile approach, was developed to prepare the GQDs. A large number of hydroxyl radicals were generated in the oxidation process and reacted effectively with the GO, which quickly and efficiently fragmented into GQDs. Well-crystallized GQDs with abundant functional groups in edge were obtained. These GQDs exhibit high scavenging efficiency and antioxidant activity for protecting dyes from oxidative degradation, which may have potential applications in medicine, cosmetics, and corrosion protection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This research was supported by the Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (2018CL04), the Sichuan Provincial Key Lab of Process Equipment and Control (GK201712) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21901174).
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2020.108288.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
